title: Future of Technology
content: Kamran Muradov's Commentary on BBC Report. Why the global technocracy model is stalling in the era of regional conflicts and recessions. Kamran Muradov's Full Article: Today, I will comment on a recent report by the BBC. According to the BBC's investigation, we will witness significant technological changes over the next 24 years. The report discusses the rapid development of quantum computers, substantial breakthroughs in nanotechnologies, technological revolutions in the medical field, traffic-free roads, and lunar travel models. It states that the events depicted in Tom Cruise's movie will cease to be mere science fiction. However, according to my commentary, the spread of these technological revolutions across the world and the establishment of a single global technocracy do not reflect reality for the current era, especially at a time when medium and small-scale wars persist in the Middle East region, monarchical regimes still rule in certain countries, and scandals like the Epstein scandal continue to unfold. In fact, a period of stagnation is currently being experienced in the field of technological revolutions. In my opinion, AI itself was the breakthrough. In the next 26 years, investments will merely be directed toward the development of existing technologies. In other words, during an era dominated by economic recessions, we are left with two options. I believe that the economic power of states, including the United States, will be deliberately weakened to pave the way for technocratic leaders like Elon Musk. Economic crises prove this to us. Nanotechnology, quantum computers, medical technological achievements, and lunar colonization issues will continue at the same pace and with a slow flow. In particular, a greater revolutionary leap in the field of artificial intelligence might only be possible toward the late 2030s with the development of microchip technology; however, even this will not be a complete revolutionary leap, but will simply follow the development paradigm of existing technologies. In short, my expectation is that in 2050, there will not be a second revolutionary turnaround that will completely surprise us, but rather predictable changes within the scope of our current expectations.

title: Systemic Negligence and Operational Malpractice at SOCAR Polymer
content: This investigation exposes critical operational failures and administrative malpractice within the SOCAR Polymer laboratory systems. Key findings include: 1. Manual Data Entry Risks: Due to persistent database 'deadlocks' and system paralysis, laboratory staff were forced to enter data manually for months, creating high risks of data corruption and entry errors. 2. Administrative Negligence: System maintenance was neglected for years, with unoptimized service brokers, massive email caches, and useless batch data overloading the infrastructure. 3. Security and Resource Sabotage: Evidence suggests limited server resources were provided intentionally to sabotage system recovery efforts. Access to critical systems was restricted and manipulated to conceal evidence. 4. HR and Contractual Fraud: Probation periods were extended illegally, and employees were forced to perform unauthorized tasks under pressure. The administration, led by Adalat Muslumov, bypassed established training and induction protocols, using personnel like Arzu Hesenova to interfere with technical duties outside their job descriptions to maintain control over a failing system.

title: Starlims System: Technical Analysis of System Tickets
content: This report details critical failures in the Starlims Laboratory Information Management System. 1. 'Page is not responding' in results entry: Caused by database deadlocks and memory leaks, leading to operational bottlenecks. 2. Starlims backup request (DEG and V1557 duplicates): API handshake failures and duplicate injections distort historical trend analysis. 3. COA Hexene-1 unloading report: Missing mandatory fields and mapping errors halt quality control. 4. Laboratory document management module: Lack of a secure module allows unauthorized protocol modifications, risking ISO 17025 accreditation. 5. QAS-analyses report email error: SMTP and firewall issues block critical alerts, delaying crisis response. 6. Missing attachments in notifications: File permission issues and storage errors prevent formal verification. 7. Crystal Report PDF export failure: Server memory (OOM) issues block large-scale documentation. 8. PP Final vs HDPE Final COA mismatch: Indicates deep database view bugs and formula corruption, threatening data integrity. These facts represent significant risks to supply chain operations and regulatory compliance.

title: Systemic Negligence and Operational Manupulation and Mobing at SOCAR Polymer
content: Starlims and SAP are computer programs. Big companies, factories, and plants use them. These programs help manage laboratories, medical equipment, money, and shipping supply chains. A laboratory worker named Irana Alimova told me something important. She said that because of a system error called a 'deadlock,' workers had to type all lab data into the computer by hand for months. The digital logs from Azercell prove this happened. A court can officially ask for these logs to see the truth. Every tech person knows how this works. When a 'deadlock' happens, the system blocks new information. The data cannot move into the database in the right order. Because the data cannot enter the system normally, the laboratory had to use manual entry (typing everything by hand). But typing everything by hand is dangerous. It creates more errors inside the system, and it can easily destroy or change the main, original data. Every ticket has a meaning. From my first day at the company, I saw that these tickets were part of a very messy and disorganized system. A company with the name SOCAR even used the Teams program's ticket system for a while. This is a disaster. For 3 months, they hid the data from me. After I insisted, they gave me data connected to the Active Directory, and maybe on purpose, connected to the PRD system and the SAP system. This data showed that the system was paralyzed. The service broker, which makes updates and manages MS SQL, was stopped. The suspicious part was that this was the reason for the email batch and email delivery errors; the email connection was broken from the data. Edalet Muslumov stated that the former ERP system administrator, Kamran Hummatov, connected all emails to one system, which created an overload. In the system, I saw new batches, useless batches, and email caches. The data was clearly not maintained and was neglected for years. Active Directory and Starlims were only used to create and close accounts, reset passwords, and set new passwords. By the order of Edalet Sexavet oglu Muslumov, Arzu Hesenova did this work, which was not in her Job Description and not active during my time, interfering with my job outside of her duties. Here, as soon as I triggered the service broker, the database did not have enough power. The system did not give an error, but it used up the server storage resources. It is clear that they gave limited resources intentionally and on purpose. They limited my free movement and trapped me by constantly scaring me and telling me 'do not touch this, do not touch that.' After 3 months, Edalet Muslumov illegally took away all my access and authority. Now I ask: If I made a mistake, why did they not give me a reprimand? Why did they not give me a warning, a reprimand, or a severe reprimand? Why were these not written in my personal employee file? Was it because they loved me? Why did Edalet Sexavet oglu Muslumov extend my 3-month probation period written in my contract, when Roman Mammadov—my line manager—did not want to extend it in the PM with an official email and written request? They did this because it was their own illegal work, and it was going to be discovered. In addition, I note that this is also proven with evidence in my email. Despite the insistence of Tural Semedli and Roman Mammadov, I stated that no induction was given to me and there was no training. I said I cannot take this risk. By the decision of Edalet Muslumov, I was forced to enter this data system under the name of PRD. Although Edalet Muslumov said we would work on the data together, he left me alone. I stayed at the office for a whole day, and the extra salary for this was not paid.

STARLIMS VS Code 
STARLIMS VS Code is an unofficial Visual Studio Code extension for working with STARLIMS Enterprise Designer assets from within VS Code. It supports browsing remote items, synchronizing them to a local workspace, reviewing changes, running supported assets, debugging forms, and combining STARLIMS workflows with Git and GitHub Copilot-aware tooling. 
Key Capabilities 
Browse, search and edit all STARLIMS code items, forms, resources, database tables, and dictionary tables directly from VS Code. 
Work with multiple STARLIMS environments through named server definitions and the built-in server selector. 
Check out items to a local workspace, compare local files with the server version, and see checked out items of other users. 
Check in, undo checkout, rename, move, add, and delete supported items. 
Export your checked out items to an SDP package. 
Run server scripts and data sources, capture script output, stream STARLIMS server logs, and clear logs from VS Code. 
Open HTML forms, debug them in Chrome or Edge, launch XFD forms through STARLIMS Bridge. 
HTML Form designer for creating or editing forms in a WYSIWYG environment with live preview, drag-and-drop controls, and property editing. 
Search by item name, perform global code search across all STARLIMS code items, and quickly navigate from source code to server scripts, client scripts, data sources via hotkeys. 
Explore database and dictionary tables, inspect table definitions, and generate SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE statements into the active editor. 
Initialize a Git repository for the local SLVSCODE mirror, configure a remote, and optionally auto-commit and auto-push local STARLIMS changes during check-in. 
Optionally detect new Git commits in the SLVSCODE repository and check matching checked-out STARLIMS script and data source files in with the Git commit message. 
Manage STARLIMS tickets (BMBH only) from VS Code by filtering queues, selecting an active ticket, undertaking, releasing, solving, and renaming tickets, and creating ticket measures during check-in. 
Launch a selected STARLIMS ticket directly into an OpenCode terminal plan session with the ticket context preloaded. 
SSL and SLSQL language support with syntax highlighting, code completion, hover documentation, go-to-definition, find references, document symbols, and folding ranges. 
Use a local MCP endpoint and Copilot-facing workspace files to enable agents to use the STARLIMS MCP for browse, search, code retrieval, checkout/check-in, runtime execution, and table operations when working with STARLIMS assets. 
Requirements 
Visual Studio Code 1.110 or later. 
Access to a STARLIMS environment. 
The SCM_API.sdp package imported into STARLIMS from the latest project release. 
The following entry in the STARLIMS web.config file: <add key="HTTPServices" value="SCM_API.*"/> 
For product development environments with system layer ID 200, enable Overwrite System Layer during package import. If your STARLIMS platform version does not support the HTTPServices setting, configure STARLIMS.urlSuffix to lims2. Importing the latest SCM_API.sdp release is strongly recommended because newer features such as ticket workflows, form callback publishing, MCP access, and backend auto-upgrade depend on current backend endpoints. 
Installation and Setup 
Install the extension in VS Code. 
Import SCM_API.sdp into your STARLIMS environment. 
Configure the STARLIMS connection settings in VS Code. 
Set STARLIMS.rootPath to the parent folder where the extension should create the local SLVSCODE workspace mirror. 
Open the STARLIMS view from the activity bar and connect to a configured server. 
The extension creates or opens the SLVSCODE folder under STARLIMS.rootPath and uses it as the local working copy for checked out items. On activation, the extension also: 
Checks the backend API version and offers to upgrade SCM_API.sdp automatically when the deployed backend is older than the extension. 
Publishes the local form callback port back to STARLIMS SCM so FormDesigner can open local code-behind files in VS Code. 
Creates .vscode/mcp.json, .github/agents/starlims.agent.md, and .github/copilot-instructions.md inside SLVSCODE when they are missing. 
Bootstraps ESLint support for STARLIMS client-side JavaScript by copying the workspace config and installing the VS Code ESLint extension when needed. 
Configuration 
Core settings: 
STARLIMS.url: STARLIMS installation URL. 
STARLIMS.user: STARLIMS user name. 
STARLIMS.userPassword: Password stored through the extension command instead of the settings file. 
STARLIMS.rootPath: Parent folder for the local SLVSCODE workspace mirror. 
Common optional settings: 
STARLIMS.servers: Named server definitions for multi-environment setups. 
STARLIMS.selectedServer: The currently active named server. 
STARLIMS.browser: Browser used for form debugging (chrome or msedge). 
STARLIMS.urlSuffix: Service suffix for environments that require lims2. 
STARLIMS.defaultFormLanguage: Default language used when checking out form items, GER by default. 
OpenCode ticket launcher settings: 
STARLIMS.opencode.command: Command or absolute executable path used by Solve ticket with OpenCode. 
STARLIMS.opencode.commandArgs: Optional leading arguments for the launcher command, such as opencode when using npx.cmd. 
STARLIMS.opencode.planModel: Initial model used when the ticket action opens OpenCode in plan mode. 
STARLIMS.opencode.buildModel: Preferred implementation model shown after the plan phase; the extension does not switch models automatically. 
STARLIMS.opencode.workingDirectory: Optional working directory override for the OpenCode terminal session. 
Git automation settings: 
STARLIMS.git.enabled: Enables Git integration for STARLIMS check-in flows. 
STARLIMS.git.autoPush: Automatically pushes Git commits after STARLIMS check-in. 
STARLIMS.git.remoteUrl: Optional remote URL used by the configure-remote command. 
STARLIMS.git.remoteName: Git remote name, defaulting to origin. 
STARLIMS.git.commitMessageGenerator: Uses either a fast local message or Copilot-assisted message generation. 
STARLIMS.git.copilotCommitMessageModel: Optional exact Copilot model name for message generation. 
Additional Git message settings are available to control detail level, prefixes, maximum length, whether item type or language is included, custom system prompts, and Copilot timeout behavior. 
Ticket Workflows 
The Tickets view groups STARLIMS tickets by status and supports title filtering, active ticket selection, ticket undertaking and release, marking tickets as solved, and renaming tickets. 
When an active ticket is selected, STARLIMS check-in commands can reuse ticket-aware reasons and automatically create ticket measures. Ticket measure text can use the same fast local generator or Copilot-assisted generation that is used for Git and check-in messages. 
The ticket context menu also includes Solve ticket with OpenCode. This opens an OpenCode terminal session in plan mode, seeds it with the selected ticket details, and reminds the user to switch to the configured build model manually once the plan is approved. For reliable Windows terminal launching, the extension writes the ticket prompt to a temporary file under the extension storage folder and feeds that content into the OpenCode CLI. 
MCP Integration 
The extension also starts a local form callback server on the first free loopback port in the range 3003-3099 for FormDesigner code-behind navigation. The selected port is published back to STARLIMS SCM so the FormDesigner client can resolve it dynamically. 
When STARLIMS.mcp.enabled is enabled, the extension exposes a local MCP endpoint on [http://127.0.0.1:3002/mcp](http://127.0.0.1:3002/mcp) by default. 
The MCP integration is intentionally limited: 
Binds to loopback only. 
Uses the currently selected STARLIMS server. 
Supports browse, search, available-language discovery, code retrieval, checkout, check-in, undo checkout, server script execution, data source execution, table definition retrieval, item creation, table creation, and edit operations. 
Extension integration tests can be triggered through MCP, but each run requires an explicit local user confirmation prompt before npm test starts. 
High-risk actions such as delete, rename, and move are still not exposed. 
Related settings: 
STARLIMS.mcp.enabled: Enables the endpoint. 
STARLIMS.mcp.port: Changes the loopback port used by MCP. 
STARLIMS.mcp.maxItems: Limits browse and search result sizes. 
STARLIMS.mcp.maxCodeCharacters: Limits code returned by read requests. 
When the local SLVSCODE workspace is created, the extension also seeds Copilot-facing helper files so agents can discover the STARLIMS MCP endpoint and prefer STARLIMS-native browse, search, code retrieval, checkout/check-in, runtime execution, and table management operations. Those instructions also tell agents to default STARLIMS form work to GER and to ask the user before running extension integration tests. 
Default Shortcuts 
F5: Run a server script or data source, or debug an HTML form, depending on the active file type. 
Ctrl+F5: Open HTML forms or XFD forms. 
Ctrl+F6: Open the HTML form designer. 
F11: Navigate to the STARLIMS item referenced at the cursor. 
Ctrl+Alt+F: Run STARLIMS global code search. 
Development 
Current development dependency floor: 
Node.js 20.19 or later, or Node.js 22.13 or later. 
Node 18 is no longer supported by the current toolchain. The locked versions of copy-webpack-plugin and jsdom require newer runtime features and will fail during CI or local builds on Node 18. 
Recommended local workflow: npm ci 
npm run lint 
npm run compile 
npm run package 
npm run compile-tests 
npm run pretest 
npm test runs the VS Code extension host tests and may fail in restricted or headless environments. To build a VSIX package on Windows, use: npm run build-windows

IN NO EVENT SHALL THE AUTHORS OR COPYRIGHT HOLDERS BE LIABLE FOR ANY CLAIM, DAMAGES OR OTHER LIABILITY, WHETHER IN AN ACTION OF CONTRACT, TORT OR OTHERWISE, ARISING FROM, OUT OF OR IN CONNECTION WITH THE SOFTWARE OR THE USE OR OTHER DEALINGS IN THE SOFTWARE.

Conflict of Interest: It is explicitly written in the document that outside activities must not create any "actual, potential or perceived conflict of interest" with the organization's work. The IT Director's attempt to force you to execute outside projects that do not exist in your employment contract is a direct conflict of interest and is considered administrative misconduct under these rules. 
Obstruction to Core Duties: According to the final clause of the document, outside activities must not impair the capacity of employees to perform their core duties to the best of their abilities. Part 2: International Labour Organization (ILO) Directives 
The conditions under which ILO officials will normally be authorized to engage in outside activities and/or accept remuneration from an outside source are clarified in the Office Directive on Rules governing outside activities and occupations. 
Office Directive, Register of financial interests and related party disclosures, IGDS No. 116 (version 1), of 1 December 2009. This applies to officials occupying positions at the D.1 level and above, the Chief of TRES/OPS, all officials in PROCUREMENT, officials at the P.5 level who exercise financial authority, and any other official whose position is so specified by the Director-General. 
ILO Circular No. 666, Series 6, entitled Employment and other types of contracts with close relatives of ILO officials, dated 4 April 2007. Under this circular, close relatives are defined as persons with close family ties to officials. These include the spouse of the official, children, the mother and father of the official, his/her brother(s), sister(s) and their spouses and children. Other close personal relations may also be relevant to the implementation of the policy and must be disclosed. 
For purposes of these disclosures, key management personnel are the Director-General, executive directors, regional directors, the Treasurer and Financial Comptroller, and the Legal Adviser. 
Office Directive, Rules governing outside activities and occupations, IGDS No. 71 (version 1), of 17 June 2009. See also Office Procedure, Approval procedures for outside activities and occupations, IGDS, No. 70 (version 1), 17 June 2009 and Office Guideline, Outside activities and occupations, IGDS No. 67 (version 1), of 17 June 2009. 
Notification broadcast by email. 
While the ILO encourages the participation of its officials in professional activities that widen knowledge and understanding of the Organization, the primary consideration that officials must bear in mind with respect to outside activities and remuneration is that the interests of the ILO must take precedence. Participation in outside activities or acceptance of outside remuneration must not involve any actual, potential or perceived conflict of interest with the work of the ILO or call into question the integrity, impartiality or independence of the official in the performance of his or her duties. Furthermore, the performance of outside activities must not impair the capacity of officials to perform their ILO duties to the best of their abilities.

"You were mainly assigned tasks that fell within your job description. Fifth Fact of Mobbing: Roman Mammadov strictly preventing my interviewee, under Adalat Sakhavat oglu Muslimov's orders, from having even a visual-only look at the STARLIMS PRD system.
Sixth Fact of Mobbing: Constantly coming up to an employee who is only in his first 1–2 weeks of a 3-month probation period every single day and demanding, "What happened, is there progress with the tickets?"
Seventh Fact of Mobbing: Assigning hidden tickets to a candidate who was still just an interviewee at the time and demanding explanations. Setting up a pre-vacancy meeting and orchestrating games behind the scenes to trap an individual into a specific point or setup is not just mobbing; it is a direct exploitation and violation of human rights.

Regarding the documentation, an explanation document was officially submitted to Human Resources and to Rovshan Najaf.

The Psychological and Personal Toll
As for the rest of the matter, it reminds us of the famous dispute of Özal's Princes in Turkey: "Does bribery have a written document, you pimp?" Now we ask: "Does mobbing have a written document, you pimp?"

Yet, we have the ultimate proof:
The Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder (PTSD) that the interviewee has suffered for the past year.
His knees collapsing as he fell down at home every single day, with an ambulance arriving every one or two weeks.
His repeated crying spells at the office, all caught on building security cameras, as well as street cameras outside.
The deterioration of his mother's health due to this immense pressure, which is fully documented in telephone conversations between Adalat Sakhavat oglu Muslimov and the interviewee's mother.
The interviewee stood directly in front of the office camera multiple times to declare these problems to his linear supervisor, Tamraz Askarov, and to Adalat Sakhavat oglu Muslimov (the IT Director from Nakhchivan—we emphasize his background intentionally to highlight the clan backing him). Unable to endure it, he wrote a resignation letter and notified Rovshan Najaf, explicitly requesting anonymity regarding the mobbing.

Instead, you wrote a reply under the guise of an "internal investigation." Adalat Muslimov then comfortably bragged, "We manipulated the server easily. Sakhavat Hasanov came to my country house in Novkhani, drank samovar tea, and apologized to me." Following this, ADALAT SAKHAVAT OGLU MUSLIMOV (from Nakhchivan) forced my interviewee to write an email, edited it, and deleted it.

My interviewee has an explicit record stored within SOCAR stating: "If I write an explanation letter, know that I was forced to write it." Faced with these absolute facts, this fraudulent Mr. Fuad Ahmadov claims there was a mutual agreement.

As legal experts, we believe that justice will punish him—if his corrupt partnerships with Farid Jafarov, who sets up billion-dollar laundering operations, allow it to happen.

Let us continue – Exposing the criminal (the indecent, immoral lies of Fuad Ahmadov). Let us look at what he states:
"Requests ('tickets') received by the IT department are entered through special software, and it is technically impossible to close those requests without resolving them in the required manner. Additionally, regarding the execution status of the said requests, it has been determined that no complaints were raised by other departments against the IT department.

Regarding the related appeal sent by you during your period of work at the Company—in May 2025—as well as the claims you raised against the Company employee R. Mammadov, it has been determined that a meeting was held with you by the Company. As a result of the meeting, the claims you raised were found to be groundless, your questions were answered properly, and in confirmation of this, you submitted a written explanation. Additionally, it appears from your direct internal correspondence with your supervisor and other related parties that the communication between you was built on mutual understanding and support. No supporting document was discovered or submitted by you to the Company regarding the allegation that the explanation taken from you concerning your previous appeal addressed to the SOCAR management was written under threat and intimidation.

In addition to the above, we would like to bring to your attention once again that the claims you shared on social networks about the Company do not reflect the truth and are slanderous. We would like to note that relevant measures will be taken by us in this regard in accordance with the legislation of the Republic of Azerbaijan.

Sincerely, 
Fuad Ahmadov, General Director 
(Official stamp of SOCAR POLYMER LLC is affixed)"

1. 'Page is not responding' in results entry: Caused by database deadlocks and memory leaks, leading to operational bottlenecks. 2. Starlims backup request (DEG and V1557 duplicates): API handshake failures and duplicate injections distort historical trend analysis. 3. COA Hexene-1 unloading report: Missing mandatory fields and mapping errors halt quality control. 4. Laboratory document management module: Lack of a secure module allows unauthorized protocol modifications, risking ISO 17025 accreditation. 5. QAS-analyses report email error: SMTP and firewall issues block critical alerts, delaying crisis response. 6. Missing attachments in notifications: File permission issues and storage errors prevent formal verification. 7. Crystal Report PDF export failure: Server memory (OOM) issues block large-scale documentation. 8. PP Final vs HDPE Final COA mismatch: Indicates deep database view bugs and formula corruption, threatening data integrity. These facts represent significant risks to supply chain operations and regulatory compliance.
